caveats: a: OEWS reports employment and wages but not task time or current automation penetration.
a: The exact-industry table excludes self-employed practitioners and may not mirror lower-middle-market group practices.
a: AI can assist clinical preparation without substituting the therapeutic encounter or licensed judgment.
rho: The AMA survey covers physicians, not this industry's practitioner population.
rho: Professional guidance establishes feasible use cases and constraints but does not measure production adoption.
rho: Privacy, consent, malpractice, and documentation-integrity requirements can turn gross time savings into review work.
e: No source measures eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm count.
e: Corporate-practice and fee-splitting rules can separate clinical ownership from management economics.
e: A group practice can meet the revenue test yet remain economically dependent on one clinician-owner or a few payor contracts.
s5: Gallup measures intentions among employer-business owners across all industries, not completed mental health practice transfers.
s5: LifeStance is much larger than screened firms and includes physician services outside the exact code.
s5: Management-service transactions or licensed-owner substitutions may not represent clean control transfers of the clinical practice.
q: LifeStance's payor mix and bargaining position are not representative of smaller practices.
q: No source measures how AI benefits are shared through mental health reimbursement or clinician compensation.
q: Capacity gains have little value where clinician supply, appointment demand, supervision, or payor panels bind.
d5: Occupational employment growth is not the same quantity as constant-quality demand for the current service basket.
d5: The principal BLS projection is for one major practitioner occupation and does not cover every profession in the code.
d5: Coverage expansions can raise billed demand while fee cuts, network closures, or workforce scarcity suppress realized visits.
o: No source directly measures year-five operator-required share for this exact industry.
o: Low-acuity coaching and structured interventions may migrate to employer, primary-care, or software channels.
o: Regulation preserves licensed accountability but does not guarantee that incumbent group practices retain the patient relationship.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patients receive scheduled in-person or virtual clinical visits; practices collect negotiated fee-for-service reimbursement from commercial and government payors and patient payments, with each counseling session generally constituting a separate service obligation.
lens.excluded_activities: Solo practices without transferable operations, physician psychiatry, inpatient and residential facilities, general outpatient centers classified elsewhere, nonclinical coaching, captive employee-assistance units, and software-only mental health products.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market private and group practices of nonphysician psychologists, counselors, marriage and family therapists, social workers, and related licensed mental health practitioners providing recurring or repeat diagnosis, psychotherapy, assessment, and behavioral treatment to external patients and payors.

a.bridge: BLS measures the exact industry's employment and wages rather than task hours: counselors and social workers, psychologists, other health practitioners, and office-administrative staff dominate the mix. I map administrative work, intake, scheduling, billing, record review, measurement, note drafting, care-plan drafting, and patient messaging to high exposure; clinical interpretation and live therapy to partial exposure; and crisis judgment, empathy, diagnosis, safeguarding, and accountability to low exposure. ACA confirms plausible administrative uses while explicitly preserving human diagnosis and treatment decisions. This may overstate exposure if privacy-safe tools cannot handle session content, and understate it if measurement-based care and documentation automation absorb more clinician time than the occupation titles reveal.
caveats: OEWS reports employment and wages but not task time or current automation penetration.
The exact-industry table excludes self-employed practitioners and may not mirror lower-middle-market group practices.
AI can assist clinical preparation without substituting the therapeutic encounter or licensed judgment.
a.change_evidence: A representative time-and-motion study of group mental health practices, wage-weighted by role and separating live care, documentation, intake, billing, scheduling, supervision, and care coordination, paired with audited preimplementation and postimplementation hours, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The practice has a meaningful clerical layer and substantial clinician documentation and synthesis, but most wage expense remains tied to licensed therapeutic interaction whose context, alliance, risk assessment, and judgment are not safely substitutable end to end.

d5.bridge: BLS projects national employment of substance abuse, behavioral disorder, and mental health counselors to grow over the coming decade, while CMS expanded independent Medicare billing to mental health counselors and marriage and family therapists and continues to pay behavioral telehealth and technology-supported services. I convert the occupation-growth signal to a moderated five-year constant-quality service-demand ratio, allowing for access expansion, unmet need, and reimbursement support but discounting clinician shortages, patient affordability, payor utilization controls, macroeconomic stress, and possible substitution by digital self-service. Employment may understate demand when vacancies persist and overstate service quantity if documentation or staffing intensity rises.
caveats: Occupational employment growth is not the same quantity as constant-quality demand for the current service basket.
The principal BLS projection is for one major practitioner occupation and does not cover every profession in the code.
Coverage expansions can raise billed demand while fee cuts, network closures, or workforce scarcity suppress realized visits.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-industry constant-price receipts and visit counts, commercial claims volumes by therapy modality, new-patient wait times, clinician vacancy rates, and payer-approved utilization per patient would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Underlying need and coverage expansion support demand, but realized visits depend on licensed supply, reimbursement, patient engagement, and the ability of group practices to fill schedules without diluting care quality.

caveats: No source measures eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm count.
Corporate-practice and fee-splitting rules can separate clinical ownership from management economics.
A group practice can meet the revenue test yet remain economically dependent on one clinician-owner or a few payor contracts.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Census product-line receipts or a commercial provider directory matched to ownership, clinician count, payor contracts, repeat-patient revenue, legal entity structure, and founder dependence for the supplied population would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The exact code is built around independent private and group practices serving external patients, so most firms in the supplied lower-middle-market population fit the service lens. The discount covers nontransferable founder practices, misclassification, mixed clinics, captive arrangements, and practices whose contracts, credentials, or patient panels cannot survive a control change.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621330 ($12,409,594,231) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621330 ($20,889,493,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $95,447 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621330); class <5: 31,706 firms, avg revenue/firm $202,692 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,025 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,993 firms, avg revenue/firm $724,532 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $114,476 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,996 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,229,971 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $194,335 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,664 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,887,960 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $456,298 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 321 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,232,732 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,142,772 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 139 firms, avg revenue/firm $19,511,748 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,082,856 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 460

caveats: No source directly measures year-five operator-required share for this exact industry.
Low-acuity coaching and structured interventions may migrate to employer, primary-care, or software channels.
Regulation preserves licensed accountability but does not guarantee that incumbent group practices retain the patient relationship.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal claims and outcomes comparing clinician-led, hybrid, and software-only mental health care, including escalation, adverse-event, retention, and payer-coverage data by acuity, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Software can support intake, psychoeducation, measurement, reminders, and low-acuity self-management, but covered diagnosis and psychotherapy remain licensed services with human responsibility. ACA explicitly preserves human diagnosis, treatment planning, contextual interpretation, and direct involvement, and CMS describes digital treatment devices as adjuncts used under an ongoing behavioral treatment plan.

q.bridge: LifeStance reports negotiated fee-for-service reimbursement and recognizes each counseling session as a performance obligation; CMS pays newly eligible mental health counselors and marriage and family therapists under the Physician Fee Schedule at a fixed relationship to psychologist payment. I translate per-service pricing into moderate retention of implemented administrative savings because payment is not mechanically cost-plus, then discount for payor renegotiation, utilization management, fee-schedule changes, clinician productivity compensation, review costs, and competitive pressure. A national platform may have better contracting leverage than lower-middle-market practices, while small specialist groups may retain savings that payors cannot observe.
caveats: LifeStance's payor mix and bargaining position are not representative of smaller practices.
No source measures how AI benefits are shared through mental health reimbursement or clinician compensation.
Capacity gains have little value where clinician supply, appointment demand, supervision, or payor panels bind.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of group-practice payor contracts and clinician compensation agreements linked to reimbursement, visit length, denial rates, documentation labor, and margins before and after AI implementation would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Per-visit reimbursement allows near-term cost savings and capacity gains to accrue to the practice, but payors set or negotiate rates and clinician compensation commonly follows visits, limiting durable capture of technology-enabled productivity.

rho.bridge: ACA identifies counseling-specific opportunities in note transcription, billing, paperwork, and intake but requires licensed oversight and rejects autonomous diagnosis or treatment planning. AMA surveyed physicians rather than nonphysician mental health practitioners; respondents strongly favored documentation and administrative uses, and operating health systems reported ambient-scribe deployment. I translate tool availability and adjacent-provider adoption into five-year implementation of exposed mental-health workflows, then discount for sensitive session data, consent, hallucinations, EHR integration, payer audits, state licensure, bias, procurement capacity, and mandatory clinician review. Physician-system evidence may overstate small-practice implementation resources, while the counseling profession's documentation burden may speed adoption.
caveats: The AMA survey covers physicians, not this industry's practitioner population.
Professional guidance establishes feasible use cases and constraints but does not measure production adoption.
Privacy, consent, malpractice, and documentation-integrity requirements can turn gross time savings into review work.
rho.change_evidence: A nationally representative survey of nonphysician mental health group practices measuring approved AI use by workflow, practice size, verified hours avoided, clinician review time, error rates, privacy incidents, and payer audit outcomes would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Ambient documentation, intake, scheduling, coding support, chart summarization, and routine communication have direct workflow fits and should diffuse, but highly sensitive data and the consequences of erroneous clinical language prevent frictionless automation.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that U.S. employer-business owners across industries planned a sale or transfer within five years, while LifeStance reports three completed outpatient mental health practice acquisitions in 2023 and none in 2024. I haircut the cross-industry intention rate for noncompletion, internal or family transfers, licensed-ownership constraints, clinician departures, nontransferable patient relationships, and the absence of acquisitions at the observed consolidator in 2024. The large-platform experience confirms transferability but may overstate buyer access to small practices; its 2024 pause may understate wider strategic and sponsor demand.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions among employer-business owners across all industries, not completed mental health practice transfers.
LifeStance is much larger than screened firms and includes physician services outside the exact code.
Management-service transactions or licensed-owner substitutions may not represent clean control transfers of the clinical practice.
s5.change_evidence: Closed transaction records from a healthcare deal database matched to U.S. outpatient mental health practice size, control percentage, seller rollover, clinician retention, and failed listings, plus owner-age data, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Group mental health practices have transferable payor contracts, clinician teams, referral channels, scheduling infrastructure, and patient access, but clinician retention and state ownership rules make a qualifying control transfer less reliable than an owner's stated intention.

principal_driver: Recurring reimbursed visits create a large base of documentation and administrative work around a durable licensed clinical service.
principal_weakness: The service and enterprise value depend on clinicians whose relationships, licenses, judgment, and continued employment are difficult to automate or guarantee after a sale.

caveats: S1 could not be reopened because the cited Census manual repeatedly timed out; its scope claim was therefore not independently verified from that source, although S2 corroborates the exact industry identity.

The a and rho bases remain judgment-heavy transformations: occupational mix and physician or professional-guidance evidence do not measure mental-health workflow hours, production adoption, or net time avoided. The PROXY labels, bridges, and broad ranges disclose this limitation.

The e and s5 estimates are not measured for lower-middle-market mental-health groups. Gallup provides broad owner intentions and LifeStance demonstrates transferability at platform scale, but neither establishes the eligible or completed-transfer shares used here.

The q base is not directly measured: fee-for-service reimbursement supports the direction of near-term retention, but the cited sources do not quantify how savings are divided among practices, clinicians, payors, and implementation or review costs.

The d5 and o inputs translate counselor employment growth and payment-policy evidence into constant-quality demand and operator-required share; neither cited source directly measures those constructs for this industry. Their PROXY and ESTIMATE treatment is appropriate but leaves substantial uncertainty.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census PDF timed out on repeated reopening attempts, so its exact text could not be independently verified in this review. The exact-industry identity is independently visible in S2, limiting the consequence of this access failure.

The exact NAICS 621330 OEWS table reports the stated clinician, psychologist, social-worker, managerial, and office-administrative employment and wage mix and discloses that self-employed workers are excluded.

The ACA submission expressly identifies transcription, billing, paperwork, and intake uses while requiring licensed review, human treatment decisions, continued direct involvement, and privacy safeguards.

The AMA article reports a physician survey emphasizing documentation and administrative uses and describes deployed ambient-scribe examples. It is adjacent-provider evidence, as the packet discloses, rather than direct evidence for nonphysician mental-health practices.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or go public within five years and provides the survey population and weighting details. This is intention evidence across industries, not completed mental-health transactions.

The LifeStance 10-K supports outpatient mental-health delivery, negotiated fee-for-service reimbursement, counseling sessions as performance obligations, visit-linked clinician productivity and compensation, acquisition strategy, three completed practice acquisitions in 2023, and none in 2024.

BLS projects 17% employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and 48,300 annual openings for substance-abuse, behavioral-disorder, and mental-health counselors, supporting the disclosed occupation-to-demand proxy.

CMS states that MFTs and MHCs may bill Medicare independently from January 1, 2024, subject to licensing and experience criteria, at 75% of clinical-psychologist payment under the Physician Fee Schedule.

The CY 2025 final-rule fact sheet supports behavioral-health coding and payment, limited telehealth flexibilities, and payment for qualifying digital mental-health treatment devices used with ongoing practitioner-led treatment plans.

run_meta.title: Offices of Mental Health Practitioners (except Physicians)

sources.supports: National occupation outlook and projected openings used as a demand proxy.

title: Offices of Mental Health Practitioners (except Physicians)
